elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit remarkable capabilities in handling multiple tasks across domains due to their emergent properties.
elements.sent-2411-002: These capabilities are further augmented during the Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) phase.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite their potential, existing work mainly focuses on domain-specific enhancements during fine-tuning, the challenge of which lies in catastrophic forgetting of knowledge across other domains.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this study, we introduce VersaTune, a novel data composition framework designed for enhancing LLMs' overall multi-ability performances during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-005: We categorize knowledge into distinct domains including law, medicine, finance, science, code.
elements.sent-2411-006: We begin with detecting the distribution of domain-specific knowledge within the base model, followed by the composition of training data that aligns with the model's existing knowledge distribution.
elements.sent-2411-007: During the fine-tuning process, weights of different domains are dynamically adjusted based on their learnable potential and forgetting degree.
elements.sent-2411-008: Experimental results demonstrate that VersaTune achieves significant improvements in multi-domain performance, with a $35.21\%$ enhancement in comprehensive multi-domain tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, in scenarios where specific domain optimization is required, VersaTune reduces the degradation of performance in other domains by $38.77\%$, without compromising the target domain's training efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-010: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become a cornerstone in Artificial Intelligence (AI)~\cite{achiam2023gpt,dwivedi2021artificial,lewkowycz2022solving , particularly for Natural Language Processing tasks~\cite{brown2020language,devlin2018bert,radford2019language , reshaping AI research and applications in domains such as law~\cite{cui2023chatlaw , medical~\cite{singhal2023large,thirunavukarasu2023large , finance~\cite{li2023large,wu2023bloomberggpt , science~\cite{beltagy2019scibert,taylor2022galactica and code~\cite{liu2024your,roziere2023code .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In order to further incentivize capabilities in these areas, LLMs typically undergo supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stages on specific datasets.
elements.sent-2411-012: As demonstrated by the robust performances of proprietary LLMs such as GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt and Gemini~\cite{team2023gemini , LLMs have the potential to master multiple tasks across all specific domains within a single model.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, most existing research on SFT tends to merely concentrate on a single ability of LLMs~\cite{dong2023abilities,xu2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-014: We try to enhance the overall multitasking performance of LLMs across various domains by optimizing data mixing ratios during the SFT phase:.
elements.sent-2411-015: Through extensive experimental analysis, we identified that the challenges associated with data composition strategies stem from the following three key aspects: C1: Catastrophic Forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-016: Given the fundamental differences between tasks across various domains, for multi-domain SFT, the sequential training strategy of specific datasets in multiple phases can easily lead to significant performance drop of prior knowledge, which is well-known as Catastrophic Forgetting~\cite{kaushik2021understanding,mccloskey1989catastrophic .}
elements.sent-2411-017: This problem hinders the versatile fine-tuning performance of a model across multiple abilities~\cite{de2021continual,dong2023abilities,yuan2022hype .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Therefore, mixing data from different domains is crucial for mitigating catastrophic forgetting during the SFT stage, enhancing the overall performance and adaptability.. C2: Low Efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-019: Existing research on data composition during the SFT phase for LLMs is still in its initial stages, with most strategies based on heuristic rules~\cite{albalak2024survey,dubey2024llama,wang2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Such approaches struggle to optimally balance different domains, failing to maximize the overall training effectiveness for multiple abilities.
elements.sent-2411-021: There lacks a well-defined methodology that efficiently enhances the versatile capabilities of LLMs across multiple domains during the SFT stage.
elements.sent-2411-022: C3: Low Flexibility in Domain Expansion.
elements.sent-2411-023: Existing SFT approaches for specific domain abilities typically pre-determine the proportions of different datasets according to prior experience~\cite{azerbayev2023llemma,roziere2023code .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Such strategies lack the flexibility to dynamically adjust the data mixing ratios of domains during the current phase based on real-time feedback from the LLMs, which hampers the ability to minimize the loss of performances in other domains as the LLMs undergo specialized training.
elements.sent-2411-025: To address these challenges, we introduce VersaTune, a novel data composition framework designed for enhancing models' overall performances across different vertical domains during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-026: We first detect the proportion distribution of domain knowledge within the target model (\Cref{subsec:knowledge_distribution_detection ), followed by data composition based on the existing distribution for multi-ability enhancement (\Cref{subsec:effective_multi_ability_fostering ) as well as flexible domain expansion (\Cref{subsec:flexible_domain_expansion ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The data composition strategy demonstrates significant improvements after fine-tuning in open-source models with parameter sizes ranging from 7B-14B, adding to the effectiveness of \ours.
elements.sent-2411-028: We evaluate our \ours on 12 downstream tasks across vertical domains, including law, medicine, finance, science, and code, as well as comprehensive datasets, each of which features two benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-029: The results show that \ours can achieve an improvement of $35.21\%$ in versatile fine-tuning across multiple domains.
elements.sent-2411-030: Furthermore, when focusing on specific-domain fine-tuning, \ours maintains the training effectiveness in the target domain while reducing the performance degradation in other non-target domains by $38.77\%$.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.984\textwidth]{figures/pipeline.pdf}} \caption{Illustration of \textbf{VersaTune}. We commence by probing the knowledge distribution within the base model $M_\theta$, utilizing a proprietary model $M_P$ to estimate the probability of sequences generated by $M_\theta$ belonging to various domains, including law, medical, finance, science, code, and others. This allows us to deduce the current knowledge domain distribution in the pretrained model $M_\theta$ through an aggregation of multiple data sample classifications. Throughout the process of efficient fine-tuning, we dynamically adjust the data domain ratios in response to $M_\theta$'s real-time performance feedback, which encompasses learnable potential and forgetting degree serving as evaluative metrics, for both overall capability enhancement and flexible domain expansion, across different training steps. } \label{fig:pipeline} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this section, we present a domain mixing strategy for fine-tuning a LLM that possesses a comprehensive multi-task capability (\Cref{subsec:knowledge_consistency_training ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: This approach is designed to align with the inherent domain knowledge distribution of the base model.
elements.sent-2411-033: Subsequently, we describe the method for detecting domain knowledge proportion of the base model, which is crucial for informing the fine-tuning process (\Cref{subsec:knowledge_distribution_detection ).}
elements.sent-2411-034: Previous research on data mixing ratios during the SFT phase for LLMs has predominantly focused on enhancing capabilities within a specific domain, often utilizing only data from that domain or employing heuristic, experience-based data proportions.
elements.sent-2411-035: We argue that such data mixing strategies can significantly impair the LLM's abilities in other domains.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the fine-tuning stage, maintaining a robust overall capability across various domains is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-037: What data mixing strategy effectively boosts the versatile performance of LLMs across different domains during the SFT phase? We propose the following statement: \hypertarget{statement1 {Statement 1 An LLM fine-tuned with domain-specific data proportions $P_{SFT(x)$ that align with its pre-trained output distributions $P_{knowledge (x)$ will exhibit enhanced and balanced performance across these domains, compared to a model fine-tuned with a non-matching data distribution.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Formally, the relationship can be represented as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:statement1} P_{SFT}(x) \approx P_{knowledge}(x), \forall x \in \chi\end{equation} where $\chi$ denotes the set of all possible data points.
elements.sent-2411-039: The statement is based on the fact that the model has developed an understanding of language features and knowledge specific to various domains during pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-040: Maintaining the same distribution of knowledge during fine-tuning allows the model to build on this pre-existing knowledge, enhancing learning efficiency and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-041: Drawing on prior research into knowledge identification methods~\cite{gekhman2024does,zhao2023knowing and training data inference strategies for LLMs~\cite{CVPR_distribution_estimation,hayase2024data , we propose a structured approach to efficiently detect domain knowledge based on statistics.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Assuming the data corpus encompasses $k$ distinct domains, as shown in \Cref{alg:domain_detection , we first prompt the base model poised for fine-tuning with the Beginning of Sequence (\textless BOS\textgreater) tokens to generate a set of $N_S$ data entries $\mathcal{S =\{s_i\}_{i=1 ^{N_S}$ (Line 3).}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Subsequently, we employ a proprietary LLM to infer the probabilities that these $N_S$ entries belong to each domain (Line 5-7).
elements.sent-2411-044: We then calculate a weighted average of the probability distributions for all data across these domains, thereby deriving the domain knowledge distribution of the current base model (Line 9).
elements.sent-2411-045: To ensure statistical robustness, the process is iteratively conducted $T$ times, and we use the mean of these $T$ iterations as the estimated result for the domain knowledge distribution.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Domain Knowledge Distribution Detection} \label{alg:domain_detection} \begin{flushleft} Input: Base model to be fine-tuned $M_{\theta}$, Proprietary model $M_P$, Hyperparameters: sample number $N_S$, maximum iterations $T$ Parameter: Data samples generated from the base model $\mathcal{S}$ Output: Domain distribution $\vec{P}$ Define $\vec{p}$: domain probability distribution of data sample $s$ \end{flushleft} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$} \STATE /* Step 1: Data Generation */ \STATE Generate data samples from base model $\mathcal{S}=\{s_i\}_{i=1}^{N_S}$ where $s_i = M_{\theta}(\textless BOS \textgreater)$ \STATE /* Step 2: Domain Probability Inference */ \FOR{each data sample $s_i$ in $\mathcal{S}$} \STATE Provide domain probability of $s_i$ referring to the proprietary model $M_P$: $\vec{p_i} = (p_{ij})_{j=1}^{k} \gets M_P(s_i)$ \ENDFOR \STATE /* Step 3: Statistics Aggregation */ \STATE Estimate the domain distribution $\vec{P}^{(t)} = (P_j^{(t)})_{j=1}^k$ where $P_j^{(t)} = \frac{1}{N_S}\sum_{i=1}^{N_S}p_{ij}$ \ENDFOR \STATE Return $\vec{P} = (P_j)_{j=1}^k$ where $P_j = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}P_j^{(t)}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-052: This value represents the highest level of domain knowledge mastery that the target model can achieve in the context of the current specific domain under given conditions.. Learnable Potential.

elements.sent-2411-054: Based on these principles, we propose \Cref{eq:loss_potential to score the learnable potential of domain $j$. \begin{equation}\label{eq:loss_potential} \gamma_j = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta}^{j} - \ell_{ref}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta}^{j}}, 0 \}\end{equation} where $\ell_{\theta ^{j}$ denotes the loss associated with the target model $M_\theta$ for the $j$-th domain, while $\ell_{ref ^{j}$ signifies the corresponding loss for the reference model $M_{ref}$ within the same domain.}}}

elements.sent-2411-056: When focusing on expanding a model to a specific domain, our objective is to minimize the loss of the model's knowledge regarding other domains.
elements.sent-2411-057: Here we segment the fine-tuning stage into $T$ distinct checkpoints.
elements.sent-2411-058: We quantify the degree of knowledge loss, or the forgetting of the current domain, by measuring the difference in loss between the $t$-th and $(t - 1)$-th training steps.
elements.sent-2411-059: This difference reflects the model's mastery loss for the tasks associated with the current domain.
elements.sent-2411-060: Based on this principle, we introduce \Cref{eq:forgetting_degree to assess the model's forgetting degree for domain $j$ at the $t$-th training step. \begin{equation}\label{eq:forgetting_degree} \varphi_j^{(t)} = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j} - \ell_{\theta^{(t - 1)}}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta^{(t - 1)}}^{j}}, 0 \}\end{equation} where $\ell_{\theta^{(t) ^{j}$ represents the loss at the $t$-th training step associated with the target model $M_\theta$ for the $j$-th domain, while $\ell_{\theta^{(t - 1) ^{j}$ denotes the loss at the preceding $(t - 1)$-th iteration for the same domain.}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{VersaTune Multi-Ability Fine-Tuning (for Domain Robustness)} \label{alg:multi_ability} \begin{flushleft} Input: Base model to be fine-tuned $M_{\theta}^{(0)}$, Domain reference loss $\{\ell_{ref}^{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$, Hyperparameters: magnitude of adjustment $\sigma$, number of training steps $T$ Parameter: Data proportion $\{P_{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ of the SFT dataset Output: Fine-tuned multi-ability model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ Define $\gamma$: learnable potential of the current domain \end{flushleft} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Initialize domain proportion $\{P_{j}^{(0)}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ according to \Cref{eq:statement1} and \Cref{alg:domain_detection} \FOR{$t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$} \FOR{$j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$} \STATE Compute learnable potential for the $j$-th domain: \quad \quad \quad \quad $\gamma_j^{(t)} = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j} - \ell_{ref}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j}}, 0 \}$ \STATE Update domain weights $P_j^{(t)'} = P_j^{(t-1)}(1 + \sigma \gamma_j^{(t)})$ \ENDFOR \STATE Renormalize domain weights: \quad \quad $P_j^{(t)} = \frac{P_j^{(t)'}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k}P_i^{(t)'}}, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, ..., k\}$ \STATE Update parameters of the fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(t)}$ \ENDFOR \STATE Return Fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-062: To cultivate the multi-tasking capabilities of a LLM during the fine-tuning phase, we have aligned the initial domain distribution in the SFT stage with the knowledge detection results of the base model as stated in \Cref{eq:statement1 .}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{VersaTune Multi-Ability Fine-Tuning (for Domain Expansion)} \label{alg:domain_expansion} \begin{flushleft} Input: Base model to be fine-tuned $M_{\theta}^{(0)}$, Domains that require enhanced cultivation $D_e$, Domain reference loss $\{\ell_{ref}^{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$, Hyperparameters: number of training steps $T$, magnitude of adjustment $\sigma $, extent of domain proportion adjustment $\delta$, variation threshold $\varepsilon$ Parameter: Data proportion $\{P_{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ of the SFT dataset Output: Fine-tuned multi-ability model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ Define $\gamma$: learnable potential of the current domain Define $\varphi$: forgetting degree of the current domain \end{flushleft} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Initialize domain proportion $\{P_{j}^{(0)}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ according to \Cref{eq:statement1} and \Cref{alg:domain_detection} \FOR{$t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$} \FOR{$j = 1, 2, \ldots, k $} \STATE Compute learnable potential for the $j$-th domain: \quad \quad \quad \quad $\gamma_j^{(t)} = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j} - \ell_{ref}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j}}, 0 \}$ \STATE Compute forgetting degree for the $j$-th domain: \quad \quad \quad \quad $\varphi_j^{(t)} = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j} - \ell_{\theta^{(t - 1)}}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta^{(t - 1)}}^{j}}, 0 \}$ \STATE Update domain weights $P_j^{(t)'} = P_j^{(t-1)}(1 + \sigma \gamma_j^{(t)})$ \ENDFOR \IF{$\frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1, j \neq e}^{k} \varphi_j^{(t)} < \varepsilon \gamma_e^{(t)}$} \STATE Update specific domain weight and renormalize: $P_j^{(t)} = \begin{cases} P_j^{(t-1)} + \delta, \text{if} \quad j = e \frac{P_j^{(t)'}}{\sum_{i=1, j \neq e}^{k}P_i^{(t)'}} (1 - P_j^{(t-1)} - \delta), \text{others} \end{cases}$ \ELSE \STATE Renormalize domain weights: \quad \quad $P_j^{(t)} = \frac{P_j^{(t)'}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k}P_i^{(t)'}}, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, ..., k\}$ \ENDIF \STATE Update parameters of the fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(t)}$ \ENDFOR \STATE Return Fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-112: The Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) stage of a Large Language Model (LLM) involves further training to refine the model's task-solving capabilities and ensure greater alignment with human instructions~\cite{zhao2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-113: While recent research has delved into exploring fine-tuning methods for multi-task enhancement~\cite{dong2023abilities,sanh2021multitask , they are still in their early stages.}
elements.sent-2411-114: However, as shown by proprietary models such as GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt and Gemini~\cite{team2023gemini , which exhibit outstanding multi-task performance, improving a model's versatile capabilities across various domains during the SFT phase is crucial.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Therefore, our work systematically investigates methods to enhance multi-domain performance during the SFT stage.
elements.sent-2411-117: Data reweighting maintains full access to the entire dataset while adjusts the relative importance of each instance for various target tasks, which is essential for both pretraining and fine-tuning stages of LLMs~\cite{wang2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-118: During the pretraining stage, DoReMi~\cite{xie2024doremi and DoGE~\cite{fan2023doge employ lightweight proxy models to estimate weights for different data sources, which are subsequently applied to the formal training of LLMs.}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Furthermore, Sheared LLaMA~\cite{xia2023sheared implements an online variant of DoReMi, where the loss reference provided by the proxy model is replaced with the state of the pre-trained model from the previous training step.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Additionally, ODM~\cite{albalak2023efficient leverages the differences in loss or perplexity across domains within a multi-armed bandit framework to dynamically adjust the data distribution along training steps.}
elements.sent-2411-121: As for the SFT phase, Dong et al.~\yrcite{dong2023abilities focus on enhancing the model's math reasoning, coding, and general human-aligning abilities through a dual-stage mixed fine-tuning strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-122: However, the mixing ratios for different domains rely heavily on enumeration and empirical methods, and the covered domains are not holistic.
elements.sent-2411-123: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the model's capabilities across various domains during the SFT stage and proposes appropriate multi-ability fine-tuning methods.
elements.sent-2411-125: Investigating the knowledge contained in current LLMs is essential for guiding their subsequent training~\cite{chang2024survey .}
elements.sent-2411-126: The knowledge encompasses multiple dimensions, such as different domain sources and task attributes.
elements.sent-2411-127: Existing work in LLM knowledge detection primarily focuses on prompting and calibration.
elements.sent-2411-128: Directly prompting the model to generate sequences and extracting confidence scores from the model~\cite{gekhman2024does,kadavath2022language,kuhn2023semantic,manakul2023selfcheckgpt is a common strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-129: However, such approaches are highly dependent on prompt design and task selection, which can introduce bias into the assessment.
elements.sent-2411-130: Other studies have attempted to infer the training data mixtures used in previous training stages of LLMs~\cite{antoniades2024generalization,hayase2024data,hu2022membership,ye2022enhanced .}
elements.sent-2411-131: The essence of these studies is to evaluate the current knowledge state of the models and provide targeted strategies for data organization in subsequent training phases.
elements.sent-2411-132: The versatile capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) across multiple domain tasks is of paramount importance.
elements.sent-2411-133: Existing fine-tuning methods for LLMs often focus on single domain tasks, leading to significant degradation in performance across other domains.
elements.sent-2411-134: In this paper, we introduce VersaTune, a data composition framework designed to enhance the multi-domain capabilities of models during the fine-tuning phase, based on the domain knowledge distribution of the target model.
elements.sent-2411-135: Experimental results from different models and downstream tasks across various domains demonstrate that \ours achieves excellent training outcomes in both scenarios of overall multi-domain enhancement and flexible expansion of specific domains.
elements.sent-2411-136: During the domain knowledge detection phase for our target models, we employed Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct\footnote{https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct as the proprietary model $M_P$.}
elements.sent-2411-137: For each target model $M_\theta$ slated for fine-tuning, we prompted the generation of $40k$ data samples using the Beginning of Sequence ($<BOS>$) token, with the sample number set at $N_S = 40,000$.
elements.sent-2411-138: These samples were subsequently assessed by the proprietary model $M_P$ to ascertain their probabilistic affinity for several vertical domains, including law, medicine, finance, science, code, and others.
elements.sent-2411-139: To ensure the reliability of our statistical outcomes, the entire process was iterated 5 times, with the maximum number of iterations set at $T = 5$.
elements.sent-2411-140: The average knowledge distribution was then computed across these iterations.
elements.sent-2411-141: Empirically, with a dataset of $40k$ samples, the distribution of sequences generated by $M_\theta$ across domains demonstrated a high degree of consistency, with an overall variance not exceeding 1.874\%.
elements.sent-2411-142: The final domain knowledge distribution for each open-source model is depicted in the stacked bar chart presented in \Cref{fig:knowledge_detection_stacked_bar_chart .}

elements.sent-2411-150: For scenarios aimed at fostering multi-ability, we trained and assessed models including LLaMA-2-7B, LLaMA-3-8B, LLaMA-2-13B, Qwen-1.5-7B, Qwen-2-7B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Qwen-2.5-14B.

elements.sent-2411-164: Large-scale pretrained models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs), have exhibited remarkable capabilities in handling multiple tasks across domains due to their emergent properties.
elements.sent-2411-165: In this study, we introduce VersaTune, a novel data composition framework designed for enhancing LLMs' overall multi-ability performances during training.
elements.sent-2411-166: We categorize knowledge into distinct domains including law, medicine, finance, science, code, etc. We begin with detecting the distribution of domain-specific knowledge within the base model, followed by the training data composition that aligns with the model's existing knowledge distribution.
elements.sent-2411-167: During the training process, domain weights are dynamically adjusted based on their learnable potential and forgetting degree.
elements.sent-2411-168: Experimental results demonstrate that VersaTune achieves significant improvements in multi-domain performance, with an $35.21\%$ enhancement in comprehensive multi-domain tasks.
elements.sent-2411-169: Additionally, in scenarios where specific domain optimization is required, VersaTune reduces the degradation of performance in other domains by $38.77\%$, while preserving the target domain's training efficacy.

elements.sent-2411-176: In recent years, large-scale pretrained models with enormous pretraining tokens and parameters, such as Transformer models, have become a cornerstone in Artificial Intelligence (AI)~\cite{achiam2023gpt,dwivedi2021artificial,lewkowycz2022solving , particularly for Natural Language Processing tasks~\cite{brown2020language,devlin2018bert,radford2019language , reshaping AI research and applications in domains such as law~\cite{cui2023chatlaw , medicine~\cite{singhal2023large,thirunavukarasu2023large , finance~\cite{li2023large,wu2023bloomberggpt , science~\cite{beltagy2019scibert,taylor2022galactica and code~\cite{liu2024your,roziere2023code .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: The success of Transformer models, such Large Language Models (LLMs), stems from their capabilities to automatically learn and distill hierarchical data representations, making them highly effective for complex tasks~\cite{nie2023angel .}
elements.sent-2411-178: In order to further enhance such abilities across these areas, LLMs typically undergo the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stages on domain-specific datasets.
elements.sent-2411-179: Data plays a fundamental role throughout the training phases of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-180: While the significance of model architectures is widely acknowledged, data-centric approaches hold substantial importance~\cite{wang2023data , which emphasizes the quality, organization, and management of data as the crucial factor for improving model performance~\cite{miao2024demystifying,fernandez2023large,data_collection_vldb .}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Efficient data management, especially in constructing an appropriately tailored training dataset with suitable data composition, is critical for synergizing the model's performance and ability to generalize across different tasks and domains during both pretraining and supervised fine-tuning stages~\cite{wang2023data,data_management4ML_sigmod .}
elements.sent-2411-182: As demonstrated by the robust performances of state-of-the-art LLMs such as GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt , PaLM~\cite{anil2023palm and Gemini~\cite{team2023gemini , LLMs have the potential to master multiple tasks across all specific domains within a single model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: However, most existing research on supervised fine-tuning tends to merely concentrate on a single ability of LLMs~\cite{dong2023abilities,xu2024large , with the multi-task performance on composite data of essentially different downstream tasks being less studied.}
elements.sent-2411-184: We try to enhance the overall multitasking performance of LLMs across various domains by optimizing data mixing ratios during training:.
elements.sent-2411-185: Given the fundamental differences between tasks across various domains, for multi-domain SFT, the sequential training strategy of specific datasets in multiple phases can easily lead to significant performance drop of prior knowledge, which is well-known as Catastrophic Forgetting~\cite{kaushik2021understanding,mccloskey1989catastrophic , as illustrated in \Cref{tab:catastrophic_forgetting and \Cref{fig:catastrophic_forgetting .}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: This problem hinders the versatile fine-tuning performance of a model across multiple domains~\cite{de2021continual,dong2023abilities,yuan2022hype .}
elements.sent-2411-187: Existing research on data composition during the supervised fine-tuning phase for LLMs is still in its initial stages, with most strategies based on heuristic or manually determined rules~\cite{albalak2024survey,dubey2024llama,wang2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-188: One of the common baselines is defining domain weights referring to the natural domain sizes, which weights all the individual data points equally.
elements.sent-2411-189: Such strategies lack the flexibility to dynamically adjust the data mixing ratios of different domains during the training process, which does not allow for real-time feedback from the LLMs in the current phase to inform and optimize the data composition.
elements.sent-2411-190: This static approach hinders the minimization of performance loss in other domains as LLMs undergo specialized training.
elements.sent-2411-191: To address these challenges, we introduce VersaTune, a novel data composition framework designed for enhancing models' overall performances across different vertical domains during supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-192: The data composition strategy demonstrates significant improvements after fine-tuning in open-source models with parameter sizes ranging from 7B-14B, adding to the effectiveness of \ours (for C1, C2 and C3).
elements.sent-2411-193: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-195: Through a quantitative analysis of the performance losses in non-target domains resulting from existing training methods, we emphasize the challenge of data composition in multi-domain task training (\Cref{sec:preliminary ).}
elements.sent-2411-197: We introduce VersaTune, a data composition framework that leverages the intrinsic domain knowledge distribution of the model to proportion training data.
elements.sent-2411-199: Our comprehensive evaluations across domains demonstrate that \ours can achieve an improvement of $35.21\%$ in versatile fine-tuning for multiple domains.
elements.sent-2411-200: Furthermore, when focusing on specific-domain fine-tuning, \ours maintains the training effectiveness in the target domain while reducing the performance degradation in other non-target domains by $38.77\%$ (\Cref{sec:experiment ).}
elements.sent-2411-201: Transformers were initially introduced to address sequence modeling and transduction challenges, including language modeling and machine translation~\cite{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-202: The core of each Transformer layer consists of self-attention mechanisms and point-wise feed-forward networks.
elements.sent-2411-203: Self-attention network enables the model to assess the importance of various words in a sequence regardless of their positions~\cite{zhang2019self .}
elements.sent-2411-204: It calculates a score for each word, indicating the degree of attention to be directed towards other sequence parts during encoding.
elements.sent-2411-205: This process involves query, key, and value weight sets that are optimized through training.
elements.sent-2411-206: Feed-forward network (FFN) applies the same linear transformation with a ReLU activation to each position individually.
elements.sent-2411-207: The FFN's role is to independently transform each position's representation, introducing non-linearity into the model and allowing it to learn complex functions~\cite{miao2022galvatron .}
elements.sent-2411-208: This component complements the self-attention mechanism by processing each input position in isolation, thus enhancing the model's adaptability to diverse data types and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-209: These operations, predominantly dense algebraic computations such as matrix multiplications, lead to significant computational expenses and memory demands.
elements.figure-2411-018: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/preliminary_versatune.pdf}} \caption{An illustration of the LLMs training workflow. In the fine-tuning phase, the model's abilities in various domains are further enhanced.} \label{fig:preliminary} \end{center}

elements.sent-2411-210: Large Language Models (LLMs) are Transformer-based models containing an extensive parameter count in the billions or greater, which are trained on a vast scale of datasets~\cite{zhao2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-211: The training process of LLMs generally involves the pretraining and fine-tuning stages.
elements.sent-2411-212: We have outlined several concepts about LLMs training.
elements.sent-2411-213: Large Language Models (LLMs) establish basic knowledge abilities, including language understanding and text generation, during the pretraining stage~\cite{brown2020language .}
elements.sent-2411-214: In this stage, LLMs engage in unsupervised training through the processing of extensive raw text corpora, thereby enhancing their capabilities in language modeling.
elements.sent-2411-215: For a given sequence $\mathrm {x = \{x_1, x_2, ..., x_n\}$, the typical task for LLMs involves the prediction of the subsequent token $x_i$ given the preceding tokens $\mathrm {x _{< i}$ as contextual input.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: The goal is to maximize the likelihood function presented in \Cref{eq:pretraining : \begin{equation}L _{LLM}^{PT}(\mathrm {x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\log{P(x_i|\mathrm {x}_{< i})} \label{eq:pretraining}\end{equation}.}
elements.figure-2411-021: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{figures/pipeline.pdf}} \caption{Illustration of \textbf{VersaTune}. We commence by probing the knowledge distribution within the base model $M_\theta$, utilizing a proprietary model $M_P$ to estimate the probability of sequences generated by $M_\theta$ belonging to various domains, including law, medicine, finance, science, code, and others. This allows us to deduce the current knowledge domain distribution in the pretrained model $M_\theta$. Throughout the process of efficient fine-tuning, we dynamically adjust the data domain ratios in response to $M_\theta$'s real-time performance feedback, which encompasses learnable potential and forgetting degree serving as evaluative metrics, for both overall capability enhancement and flexible domain expansion, across different training steps. } \label{fig:pipeline} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-217: It can be thought of as a special marker that indicates the start of a new sequence, allowing the model to reset its context and begin processing a new piece of text.
elements.sent-2411-218: In the context of pretraining, the <BOS> token is used to initialize the input to the model, and it can be concatenated with the actual text data to form the input sequence.
elements.sent-2411-219: This token helps the model to differentiate between the start of a new input and the continuation of an existing one.
elements.sent-2411-220: It is particularly crucial in tasks where the model needs to generate text or understand the beginning of a new sentence or document, which helps the model to learn the boundaries of text sequences and to better model the statistical properties of the language data it is trained on.
elements.sent-2411-221: The use of <BOS> tokens, along with other special tokens like <EOS> (End of Sequence), helps the model to learn the boundaries of text sequences and to better model the statistical properties of the language data it is trained on.
elements.sent-2411-222: The Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) stage involves further training on the pretrained model to refine it task-solving capabilities and ensure greater alignment with human instructions across multiple domains~\cite{zhao2023survey,nie2023angel .}
elements.sent-2411-223: During the SFT phase, it is a typical practice to employ datasets specific to a particular domain for the fine-tuning of LLMs, which may lead to a decline in performance on non-target domains, a phenomenon commonly referred to as catastrophic forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-224: We conducted experiments on open-sourced models including LLaMA~\cite{dubey2024llama,touvron2023llama,touvron2023llama2 and Qwen~\cite{bai2023qwen,yang2024qwen2 series to assess how the model's proficiency in other domains changes when fine-tuned with data from a single domain, as illustrated in \Cref{tab:catastrophic_forgetting and \Cref{fig:catastrophic_forgetting .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: We have regulated the number of training instances per epoch to a fixed count of 10,000.
elements.sent-2411-226: More details about training and evaluation settings can be found in \Cref{subsec:experimental_setup .}
elements.sent-2411-227: Our findings indicate that when a model is trained exclusively with data from a single domain, its performance on tasks from other domains tends to degrade progressively over the course of training.
elements.sent-2411-228: Therefore, our work systematically investigates methods to enhance multi-domain performance during the SFT stage to bridge this gap.
elements.sent-2411-229: In this section, we introduce VersaTune, a novel data composition framework designed for multi-capability training.
elements.sent-2411-230: This framework aims to effectively compose data from multiple domains and optimize the data composition during training.
elements.sent-2411-231: Here, we first present a domain mixing strategy for fine-tuning a LLM that possesses a comprehensive multitask capability (\Cref{subsec:knowledge_consistency_training ).}
elements.sent-2411-232: This approach is designed to align with the inherent domain knowledge distribution within the base model waiting for subsequent training.
elements.sent-2411-233: Following this, we describe the method for detecting domain knowledge proportion of the base model, which is crucial for informing the fine-tuning process (\Cref{subsec:knowledge_distribution_detection ).}
elements.sent-2411-234: What data mixing strategy effectively boosts the versatile performance of LLMs across different domains during the SFT phase? We propose the following statement: \hypertarget{statement1 {Statement 1 (Knowledge Consistency Training).}}
elements.sent-2411-235: An LLM fine-tuned with domain-specific data proportions $P_{SFT(x)$ that align with its pretrained output distributions $P_{knowledge (x)$ will exhibit enhanced and balanced performance across these domains, compared to a model fine-tuned with a non-matching data distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: The rationale behind this statement is rooted in the observation that during the pretraining phase, LLMs develop a general understanding of language features and domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-237: By maintaining the same distribution of knowledge during fine-tuning, the model can build upon this pre-existing knowledge, thereby enhancing learning efficiency and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-238: The process involves the generation of textual outputs from the base model $M_{\theta}$ waiting to be fine-tuning, followed by their classification into predefined domains referring to a proprietary model $M_P$.
elements.sent-2411-239: The above process is repeated multiple times to ensure statistical robustness.
elements.sent-2411-240: Assuming the data corpus encompasses $k$ distinct domains, as shown in \Cref{alg:domain_detection , we first prompt the base model poised for fine-tuning $M_{\theta}$ with the \hyperlink{BOS {Beginning of Sequence (\textless BOS\textgreater) tokens to generate a set of $N_S$ data entries $\mathcal{S =\{s_i\}_{i=1 ^{N_S}$ (Line 3).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-241: Subsequently, we employ a proprietary model $M_P$ to infer the probabilities that these $N_S$ entries belong to each domain (Line 5-7).
elements.sent-2411-242: We then calculate a weighted average of the probability distributions for all data across these domains, thereby deriving the domain knowledge distribution of the current base model $M_{\theta}$ (Line 9).
elements.sent-2411-243: Our goal is to construct a composite dataset covering $k$ specific domains, which can be denoted as $\mathcal{D _{train = \{ (D_{train ^j, P_j)\}_{j=1 ^{k}$ with each tuple representing a specific domain and its corresponding proportion, such that training a model on dataset $\mathcal{D _{train}$ could achieve overall lower loss on a uniformly distributed composite target validation dataset $\mathcal{D _{val = \{ (D_{val ^j, 1 / k)\}_{j=1 ^{k}$ or meet the flexible domain expansion while preserving the performance of other domains.}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-244: This value represents the highest level of domain knowledge mastery that the model can achieve in the context of the current specific domain under given conditions.. Learnable Potential.
elements.sent-2411-245: Based on these principles, we propose \Cref{eq:loss_potential to score the learnable potential of domain $j$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:loss_potential} \gamma_j = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta}^{j} - \ell_{ref}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta}^{j}}, 0 \}\end{equation} where $\ell_{\theta ^{j}$ denotes the loss associated with the target model $M_\theta$ for the $j$-th domain, while $\ell_{ref ^{j}$ signifies the corresponding loss for the reference model $M_{ref}$ within the same domain.}}}

elements.sent-2411-246: When conducting fine-tuning on a pretrained model, there are instances where we aim to particularly enhance the model's performance on specific domain tasks.
elements.sent-2411-247: As outlined in \Cref{alg:domain_expansion , we initially establish the data distribution based on the knowledge detected from the original pretrained model (Line 1).}
elements.sent-2411-248: At each training step $t$, we calculate the learnable potential and forgetting degree scores for each domain (Line 4-5), and assign domain weights for the current training phase following the method from \Cref{alg:multi_ability (Line 6).}
elements.sent-2411-249: A trade-off is necessary between the remaining learning margin of the domain that requires focused cultivation and the model's forgetting degree towards other non-target domains: if the improvement benefit of the specific domain exceeds the average forgetting degree of the other domains (ratio greater than $\varepsilon$), we increase the data weight of the current specific domain by $\delta$, and proportionally reduce the weights of the other non-target domains according to \Cref{eq:statement2_cases (Line 8-9); otherwise, we maintain the current domain distribution and only perform minor adjustments and renormalization as described in \cref{alg:multi_ability (Line 10-11).}}

elements.sent-2411-264: We have collected datasets spanning 6 domains for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), including Lawyer-Instruct\footnote{https://huggingface.co/datasets/Alignment-Lab-AI/Lawyer-Instruct , the training portion of MedQA~\cite{jin2020disease , Finance Alpaca\footnote{https://huggingface.co/datasets/gbharti/finance-alpaca , Sonnet3.5 Science Conversations\footnote{https://huggingface.co/datasets/jeffmeloy/sonnet3.5\_science\_conversations , Code Alpaca\footnote{https://github.com/sahil280114/codealpaca and Alpaca~\cite{taori2023stanford , denoted as $\mathcal{D _{train = \{ (D_{train ^j, P_j)\}_{j=1 ^{6}$, to represent SFT datasets with respect to law, medicine, finance, science, code as well as general capabilities.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: In order to prevent domain overlap, we curated the Alpaca dataset by excluding data pertaining to the other specific five domains, keeping only the general domain instances unrelated to them.
elements.sent-2411-266: Details of the training datasets can be found in \Cref{tab:datasets .. Model Details.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Regarding reference models, for the LLaMA series, we used the Sheared-LLaMA-1.3B~\cite{xia2023sheared as a lightweight reference model; as for the Qwen series, we utilized Qwen-2-1.5B and Qwen-2.5-1.5B as our reference models.. Training Details.}
elements.sent-2411-268: All experiments were conducted based on full-parameter fine-tuning, during which we utilized a learning rate scheduler featuring linear warm-up and cosine decay, peaking at a learning rate of 2e- 5, alongside a warmup ratio of 0.03, a weight decay of 0.0 and a batch size of 128 for 4 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-269: To maintain consistency, the total volume of training data across domains was controlled to 60,000 per epoch, with each domain's samples being downsampled or upsampled according to the corresponding weights during the mixing process.
elements.sent-2411-270: We conducted all fine-tuning and evaluation experiments on NVIDIA RTX H800.. Benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-271: We evaluate the performance of the models on downstream tasks across various domains, using two relevant benchmarks for each domain.
elements.sent-2411-272: For the MMLU dataset, we selected 14 sub-tasks to construct the MMLU-Sci subset~\cite{zhang2024sciglm for testing, aiming to ensure a robust and thorough evaluation.}

The background color of the radar chart signifies the domain to which the current benchmark belongs, with reference to the color key provided in \Cref{fig:pipeline}, which includes law, medicine, finance, science, code, and general fields.} \label{fig:multi_ability_radar_chart} \end{center}

elements.figure-2411-027: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/medicine_domain_expansion.pdf}} \caption{ Domain expansion results, where we take the medicine domain as an example. We evaluated the checkpoints from each epoch. Left (a) presents the grouped stacked bar chart showing the growth or loss of capabilities in non-target domains compared to the pre-fine-tuning state. Within each group, the left, center, and right bars represent: (1) 100\% specific domain fine-tuning, (2) domain increase with uniform distribution of remainder, and (3) \ours implementation based on \Cref{alg:domain_expansion}. Right (b) features the line chart depicting the enhancement of the medicine domain's capabilities relative to the pre-fine-tuning state.} \label{fig:domain_expansion_medicine} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-274: Compared to the uniform distribution of data across domains, \ours enhances downstream task performances by $35.21\%$, which further underscores the effectiveness of our data composition strategy for enhancing the model's overall multi-domain capabilities during the supervised fine-tuning phase.
elements.sent-2411-275: Since we have not conducted domain-specific refinement for domains outside the current five specific domains, the models' performance gains on general benchmarks are not as noticeable.
elements.sent-2411-276: For domain expansion scenarios, \ours has nearly maintained training efficiency while reducing the model's loss of competencies in other domains by $38.77\%$ comparing to 100\% specific domain fine-tuning, as depicted in \Cref{tab:flexible_domain_expansion and \Cref{fig:domain_expansion_medicine , where we averaged the experimental results from LLaMA-3-8B and LLaMA-2-13B.}}
elements.sent-2411-277: Three interesting phenomena are observed from the outcomes:.
elements.sent-2411-279: Notably, by the second epoch of training, the degradation in performance across non-target domains tends to be mitigated to some extent, and in some cases, there is even a positive trend in capability enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-280: We attribute this phenomenon to the fact that the absolute number of samples for each domain, relative to the distribution of domain samples, has a predominant influence on model performance at this stage.
elements.sent-2411-281: Domains are not entirely orthogonal to each other, and there is a degree of mutual reinforcement among them.
elements.sent-2411-282: For instance, enhancing capabilities in the medicine domain can, to a certain degree, boost performance in the science domain, as illustrated in \Cref{fig:domain_expansion_medicine .}
elements.sent-2411-284: From the slope of the target domain performance increase in \Cref{fig:domain_expansion_medicine (b), it is evident that the model's efficiency in mastering knowledge of a specific domain diminishes over training.}
elements.sent-2411-285: In other words, as training progresses, the model's grasp of the current target domain approaches saturation, while its performance on non-target domains declines sharply.
elements.sent-2411-286: Consequently, greater emphasis should be placed on mitigating losses in non-target domains during this phase, aiming to strike a balance between domain expansion and the salvage of capabilities in non-target domains, which is also demonstrated in \Cref{fig:domain_expansion_ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-287: In \Cref{tab:multi_ability_total and \Cref{fig:multi_ability_radar_chart , we also present the experimental results of data composition strategies that allocate domain data in a manner inversely proportional to the pre-existing knowledge distribution detected within each domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-288: As expected, the inverse distribution strategy yielded even lower$\color{red \downarrow $ performance compared to the simplest approach of uniform distribution, which evenly distributes data across all domains.}
elements.sent-2411-289: This finding underscores the importance of aligning domain data ratios with the inherent knowledge distribution of the model during training, which proves the efficacy of knowledge consistency training stated in \Cref{subsec:knowledge_consistency_training .}
elements.sent-2411-290: During the process of cultivating multiple capabilities, we compared \ours with fixed domain weights referring to the knowledge distribution obtained from probing the target model $M_\theta$ prior to supervised fine-tuning, namely \ours Constant, to ablate the component of dynamic adaptation in \Cref{alg:multi_ability .}
elements.sent-2411-291: A key reason for this phenomenon is the distribution of domain knowledge mastered by the model changes during training, and the learning efficiency varies among domains.
elements.figure-2411-028: \begin{center} \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/domain_expansion_ablation.pdf}} \caption{Line chart of the multi-task performances of models across different domains during the domain expansion process. We calculated the average percentage change for both target and non-target domains comparing to the initial state. Additionally, we highlighted the performance changes of the \ours at various checkpoints using green numerical annotations.} \label{fig:domain_expansion_ablation} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-292: We consider conducting a comparative analysis between the outcomes of \ours and those implementing an unconditional dynamic increase of the specific domain, where we remove the implementation of Line 8 in \Cref{alg:domain_expansion , to ablate the component of criteria for determining the upper limit of domain expansion in \Cref{alg:domain_expansion .}}
elements.sent-2411-293: In \Cref{fig:domain_expansion_ablation , we present the trends in the overall multi-domain performance of the models under specific domain expansion for each domain.}
elements.sent-2411-294: It can be observed that, for the majority of domains, the gap in average multi-task performance between models trained with \ours and those without an upper limit on domain proportion becomes increasingly pronounced after the second or third epoch.
elements.sent-2411-295: We speculate that it is because by the later stages of supervised fine-tuning, models' proficiency in the target domain approaches saturation.
elements.sent-2411-296: Notably, between the second and third epochs of fine-tuning, the model reaches a balance where the efficiency of improvement in the target domain is matched by the rate of performance degradation in non-target domains.
elements.sent-2411-297: The finding shows that the criteria for determining the upper limit on the proportion of a specific domain during domain expansion, has mitigated the loss of capabilities in other domains experienced by the target model $M_\theta$ during the fine-tuning process.
elements.sent-2411-298: As for the SFT phase, Dong et al.~\cite{dong2023abilities focus on enhancing the model's math reasoning, coding, and general human-aligning abilities through a dual-stage mixed fine-tuning strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-299: The essence of these studies is to evaluate the current knowledge state of the models and provide targeted strategies for data organization and management in subsequent training phases.
elements.sent-2411-300: Large-scale pretrained models, such as Large Language Models (LLMs), have demonstrated exceptional performance across multiple domains due to their emergent properties.
elements.sent-2411-301: Versatile capability across multiple domains is of paramount importance, which is typically cultivated during the Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) phase of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-302: However, existing data composition methods for the SFT stage of LLMs often focus on single domain tasks, leading to significant degradation in performance across other domains.
elements.sent-2411-303: Therefore, there is an urgent need for an effective data composition strategy during the SFT phase.
elements.sent-2411-304: In this paper, we introduce VersaTune, a data composition framework designed to enhance the multi-domain capabilities of models during the supervised fine-tuning phase, which is based on the domain knowledge distribution of the target model.
elements.sent-2411-305: Experimental results from different models and downstream tasks across various domains demonstrate that \ours achieves excellent training outcomes in both scenarios of overall multi-domain enhancement and flexible expansion of specific domains.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Knowledge Distribution Detection} \label{alg:domain_detection} \begin{flushleft} Input: Base model $M_{\theta}$, Proprietary model $M_P$, Hyperparameters: sample number $N_S$, maximum iterations $T$ Parameter: Data samples $\mathcal{S}$ generated from $M_{\theta}$ Output: Domain distribution $\vec{P}$ Define $\vec{p}$: domain probability distribution of data sample $s$ \end{flushleft} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \FOR{$t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$} \STATE /* Step 1: Data Generation */ \STATE Generate data samples from the base model: $\mathcal{S}=\{s_i\}_{i=1}^{N_S}$ where $s_i = M_{\theta}(\textless BOS \textgreater)$ \STATE /* Step 2: Domain Probability Inference */ \FOR{each data sample $s_i$ in $\mathcal{S}$} \STATE Provide domain probability of $s_i$ referring to the proprietary model $M_P$: \quad \quad $\vec{p_i} = (p_{ij})_{j=1}^{k} \gets M_P(s_i)$ \ENDFOR \STATE /* Step 3: Statistics Aggregation */ \STATE Estimate domain knowledge distribution: \mbox{$\vec{P}^{(t)} = (P_j^{(t)})_{j=1}^k$ where $P_j^{(t)} = \frac{1}{N_S}\sum_{i=1}^{N_S}p_{ij}$} \ENDFOR \STATE \mbox{Return $\vec{P} = (P_j)_{j=1}^k$ where $P_j = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T}P_j^{(t)}$} \end{algorithmic}
elements.subsubsection-2411-009: Preliminary: Learnable Potential and Forgetting Degree of Knowledge
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{VersaTune Multi-Ability Fine-Tuning (for Domain Robustness)} \label{alg:multi_ability} \begin{flushleft} Input: Base model to be fine-tuned $M_{\theta}^{(0)}$, Domain reference loss $\{\ell_{ref}^{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$, Hyperparameters: adjustment magnitude $\sigma$, training step number $T$ \mbox{Parameter: Data proportion $\{P_{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ of dataset} Output: Fine-tuned multi-ability model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ Define $\gamma$: learnable potential of the current domain \end{flushleft} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Initialize domain proportion $\{P_{j}^{(0)}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ according to \Cref{eq:statement1} and \Cref{alg:domain_detection} \FOR{$t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$} \FOR{$j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$} \STATE Learnable potential for the $j$-th domain: \quad \quad $\gamma_j^{(t)} = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j} - \ell_{ref}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j}}, 0 \}$ \STATE Update domain weights: \quad \quad $P_j^{(t)'} = P_j^{(t-1)}(1 + \sigma \gamma_j^{(t)})$ \ENDFOR \STATE Renormalize domain weights: \quad $P_j^{(t)} = \frac{P_j^{(t)'}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k}P_i^{(t)'}}, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, ..., k\}$ \STATE \mbox{Update parameters of fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(t)}$} \ENDFOR \STATE Return Fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-374: In this work, we introduce VersaTune, a noval data composition framework designed to enhance the multi-domain capabilities of models during the supervised fine-tuning phase of LLMs, which is based on the domain knowledge distribution of the target model.
elements.sent-2411-375: Experimental results have demonstrated that \ours achieves excellent training outcomes in both scenarios of overall multi-domain enhancement and flexible domain expansion.

elements.sent-2411-378: Additionally, when computing the learnable potential and forgetting degree of knowledge, to balance the computational cost and effectiveness, we employ a lightweight proxy model to for calculation, yet it does not fully represent the performance tendencies of the target model during actual evaluating.
elements.sent-2411-379: Integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) into domain reweighting settings holds potential for improving multi-domain capabilities of models, while it also brings several ethical considerations that must be addressed to ensure responsible and beneficial use.
elements.sent-2411-380: Additionally, \ours adhere to privacy standards by merely utilizing open-sourced datasets, ensuring that personal data used in the training process is anonymized and securely handled to protect individual privacy rights.
elements.sent-2411-381: In this section, we provide the background information and design motivation for our VersaTune.

elements.sent-2411-392: A trade-off is necessary between the remaining learning margin of the domain that requires focused cultivation and the model's forgetting degree towards other non-target domains.
elements.sent-2411-393: If the improvement benefit of the specific domain exceeds the average forgetting degree of the other domains (ratio greater than $\varepsilon$), we increase the data weight of the current specific domain by $\delta$, and proportionally reduce the weights of the other non-target domains according to \Cref{eq:statement2_cases (Line 8-9).}
elements.sent-2411-394: Otherwise, we maintain the current domain distribution and only perform minor adjustments and renormalization as described in \cref{alg:multi_ability (Line 10-11).}
elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{VersaTune Multi-Ability Fine-Tuning (for Domain Expansion)} \label{alg:domain_expansion} \begin{flushleft} Input: Base model to be fine-tuned $M_{\theta}^{(0)}$, Domains that require enhanced cultivation $D_e$, Domain reference loss $\{\ell_{ref}^{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$, Hyperparameters: number of training steps $T$, magnitude of adjustment $\sigma $, extent of domain adjustment $\delta$, variation threshold $\varepsilon$ \mbox{Parameter: Data proportion $\{P_{j}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ of the SFT dataset} Output: Fine-tuned multi-ability model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ Define $\gamma$: learnable potential of the current domain Define $\varphi$: forgetting degree of the current domain \end{flushleft} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Initialize domain proportion $\{P_{j}^{(0)}\}_{j=1}^{k}$ according to \Cref{eq:statement1} and \Cref{alg:domain_detection} \FOR{$t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$} \FOR{$j = 1, 2, \ldots, k $} \STATE Learnable potential for the $j$-th domain: $\gamma_j^{(t)} = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j} - \ell_{ref}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j}}, 0 \}$ \STATE Forgetting degree for the $j$-th domain: $\varphi_j^{(t)} = \max \{\frac{\ell_{\theta^{(t)}}^{j} - \ell_{\theta^{(t - 1)}}^{j}}{\ell_{\theta^{(t - 1)}}^{j}}, 0 \}$ \STATE Update domain weights: $P_j^{(t)'} = P_j^{(t-1)}(1 + \sigma \gamma_j^{(t)})$ \ENDFOR \IF{$\frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1, j \neq e}^{k} \varphi_j^{(t)} < \varepsilon \gamma_e^{(t)}$} \STATE Update specific domain weight: {$P_j^{(t)} = \begin{cases} P_j^{(t-1)} + \delta, \text{if} \quad j = e \frac{P_j^{(t)'}}{\sum_{i=1, j \neq e}^{k}P_i^{(t)'}} (1 - P_j^{(t-1)} - \delta), \text{others} \end{cases}$} \ELSE \STATE Renormalize domain weights: $P_j^{(t)} = \frac{P_j^{(t)'}}{\sum_{i=1}^{k}P_i^{(t)'}}, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, ..., k\}$ \ENDIF \STATE \mbox{Update parameters of fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(t)}$} \ENDFOR \STATE Return Fine-tuned model $M_{\theta}^{(T)}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-405: Furthermore, to demonstrate the efficacy of \ours across diverse domain tasks, we conducted a comparative analysis between Qwen-2.5-32B + VersaTune and frontier models across various domain-specific tasks, with results visualized in \Cref{fig:sota_comparison_radar .}
elements.sent-2411-406: Such experimental results indicate that Qwen-2.5-32B equipped with \ours enhances multi-domain performance to the state-of-the-art levels, which even outperforms frontier models like GPT-4o, Claude3.5-Sonnet and DeepSeek-V3 by 0.86\%, 4.76\% and 4.60\% on the overall domain capabilities, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-407: This comparison underscores the superior performance of our \ours in advancing multi-domain capabilities.
